calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate Renewal Of Judgment
Off Calendar Jan-23-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT LAURA KERIMOVA's Motion To Vacate Renewal Of Judgment.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Laura Kerimova's motion to vacate renewal of judgment is granted.

Defendant Dickinson Financial LLC won a money judgment against Kerimova in 2011. The judgment was renewed in 2021 and notice of renewal was mailed in 2022. Now, over a year later, Kerimova moves to vacate the renewal of judgment on grounds she was never served with the underlying summons and complaint.

A judgment debtor may challenge the renewal of judgment on any ground that would be a defense to an action on the judgment. (CCP 683.170(a).) The judgment debtor bears the burden of proving, by the preponderance of the evidence, they are entitled to this relief. (Fidelity Creditor Serv., Inc. v. Browne (2001) 89 Cal.App.4th 195, 199.) Failure to properly serve the summons and complaint is one such ground. (Id. at 202.) This challenge must be made not later than 30 days after service of the notice of renewal. (CCP 683.170(b).) A judgment debtor who claims they were not served with the notice of renewal bears the burden of proving so. (See American Contractors Indemnity Co. v. Hernandez (2022) 73 Cal.App.5th 845, 849.)

First, Kerimova contends her motion is timely because she was never served with the notice of renewal of judgment. Here, Dickinson's proof of service accompanying its notice of renewal claims that papers were mailed to a Sacramento address. Yet the Kerimova declaration provides she no longer lived at this address at the time of service. (Kerimova declaration, para. 8.) Kerimova also submits a letter from a leasing office in Sacramento that corroborates her claim she had, by then, relocated to San Francisco. (Kerimova declaration, exh. A.) Considering this evidence, Kerimova has met her burden of showing she did not receive service of the renewal judgment.

Second, Kerimova has rebutted any presumption that she was properly served with the summons and complaint. CCP 415.20 authorizes substitute service upon a "competent member of the household." Here, the proof of service of summons provides the papers were left with a co-occupant named Niko. Dickinson submits no further evidence Niko was a competent member of the household. In turn, the Kerimova declaration provides she only lived with her mother at that time, no men lived with her, and she does not know any men named Niko. (Kerimova declaration, para. 5.) Kerimova has met her burden of showing she was not served with the summons and complaint and is thus entitled to relief.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: S. Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)
